speaker: interviewer
text: The date is May 6th, 1996. The survivor is Sydney Schwimmer and this is tape number eight. Mr. Schwimmer, would you introduce your wife to us and tell us when you were married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this is Agnes Schwimmer. She used to be Agnes Birnbaum. She was also born in the Carptahian, not far from us, from Dovhoje. It's a place by the name of Svalyava or Szolyva, very nice place. A great little town than Dovhoje. And she came out to United States, I believe, in 1947. But believe it or not, I remember her when she was 10 years old. I had a brother to live there and I remember her father and mother. And I may have [? even ?] been in their house once but I never thought that she was going to be my wife. I'm a little bit older than her.

speaker: interviewee
text: So finally, we met in America. This was the destiny. We married here in December 17, 1950. And we lived a great part in Brooklyn. We have two daughters, very lovely daughters. Both live in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: One of my daughters, Miriam, has four boys. One is already 19, then she has 17, 13, 8. And my younger daughter, Deborah, Debbie, she has two daughters, beautiful daughters. As a matter of fact, yesterday was the birthday on the 5th of May, one of my granddaughters. Her name is Renee and the other my granddaughter, is--

speaker: interviewee
text: Michelle and she has the same birthday as I, December the 14, except not the same year. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father, Jacob Schwimmer. He was born in 1882, and this picture probably was taken in 1916 or '17, before the end of World War I. Now, my father went to Auschwitz at the age of 63. He perished in Auschwitz on May the 18, 1944. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] This is my sister, Dvora, and she was our youngest. She was about age 23 when she went to Auschwitz in 19-- in May 18, 1944. However, she was selected to go to work. At one point, in September 1944, before they were working somewhere in Germany in a munitions factory, many girls in the hundreds. And the English bombarded that place, and she perished in that place.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- this picture, I don't exactly-- it was taken during the Hungarian regime, maybe 1942, '43. And as you can see, she was playing on a violin. She was a very good violin player. She was a very good musician, self-- learned her self. And this is in the backyard of our yard from home. This is the only picture which remains in my possession.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my niece, my sister, Hannah's little girl. She was about seven years old when she went to Auschwitz. She perished also in Auschwitz approximately the same time as Vee, in 1944 on May 18 or 19. She had more children. She had another child, also never returned and she herself, the mother, also perished. This picture only I did get in the United States, a small one from someone who had this picture from-- we came out to the United States before the war. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture of myself. I was going to the Sixth Gymnasium in Beregsaz. It was made approximately 1934. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a picture of myself with my brother. This picture was made in Brno, Czechoslovakia, when I went to the university. It was made about in 1938 in October or November. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a picture of myself, as you can see. And I am here in a Hungarian uniform. When I was discharged from labor camp in 1944 around February in Mohacs, a photographer came in. And I made that picture something for the purpose maybe someday I'm going to use it to falsify some identification in case I need to travel. I didn't know exactly at that time that I will use it later on for the-- for a certain purpose. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] As you can see, this is a picture from Birkenau. It's taken out from the Auschwitz Album, page number 19. At one point, which I remember now, they took a group of inmates who already were there for two or three days and took them to this place when the new arrivals came. And so I don't remember exactly that I was there. I mean, from a standpoint that I would-- I would say that I know exactly where they took us. The only thing I took-- they took out from our group several people and this was for the purpose to help with the new arrivals when they came, all kinds of help. And so I was among that group. At a certain point, you can see my face. I'm standing right near an SS man. Actually, I found out only this after I have seen the picture of Auschwitz-- I mean, the album of Auschwitz. It's unquestionable myself because I have a similar picture, not in uniform of inmate, which exactly looks like me and now I definitely remember that it was me who was there. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] As you can see, from the top of this picture an arrow is going down and it's going toward my face and I'm standing near an SS man. So this is the picture of myself as I was assigned to some work there with many other inmates. And right-- I am right there near this SS man. I remember exactly that they took us to [INAUDIBLE]. I didn't realize that someone or somewhere they make a picture. And this only I found out when I have seen the Auschwitz Album or this place where they call it Birkenau. This probably was done around May 20, May 21, 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a Hungarian dog tag. I spoke about it. What's important, as you can see, you have on the string two knots. When I went through the water, the immersion in Auschwitz, the SS man cut it off with a scissor but I took it back from the floor and made another knot, as you can see it there. Now below that, you can see there is this identification, which they gave out in Hungarian labor camp. Not for everyone, I was privileged. And it's been folded in four folds. This is now put in one fold. This how it looked when it was inside and I was wearing it all the time. [INAUDIBLE] As you can see, on the other side this dog tag, which was issued in 1942, has a stamp and signature by an officer but it has been soaked in the water as I passed by the immersion. I immersed in the water in Auschwitz as I told the story. So you still can see that the stamp is still there but it's faded. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yeah, this is a family picture made in 1990 at the occasion of my first grandson's bar mitzvah. It was made in Jerusalem at the Kotel Ma'aravi, the Wailing Wall. As you can see, from left to right, first is my daughter, my older daughter, Miriam Haber, her name is now Haber. The second one is my younger daughter, Debbie Mark, also lives in Israel. Then next, comes my wife, Agnes Schwimmer. Near her is my grandson, Meir Moshe Haber, whose bar mitzvah it was. And then it's myself. You know my name already. Near myself is standing my grand-- my son-in-law. His name is Israel Haber, and he's the husband of Miriam Haber. He is holding a little child by the name of Menachem. We're going now from the right. From the right is Yonatan-- no, Yitzchak, Yitzchak Haber. Then in the middle a little bit further is Menachem Haber. Then are the two girls, which are my grandchildren from my daughter, Debbie Mark. And her-- named the younger, is Michelle Mark and the other is Renee Mark. Both live in Israel in [INAUDIBLE]. My older daughter lives in Ramat Hagolan, right near the Syrian border. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] This is a picture of myself issued for the purpose of identification. Now, during the time of the Hungarian regime, a Jew, if he wanted to travel or go any place, he had to have identification. And this was issued in 1941 in Dovhoje. And a, a stamp has been made by the community of Dovhoje and signed by the secretary by the name of Drohovich. This, almost every Jew had to have identification if he wanted to go someplace else and-- in the train or some other places if they wanted to see who he is.
